description: A blog about software development, AI, and technology

items.content_html:
It's Been A While

Hello, everyone. It has been quite some time since the last time I wrote a post. So much has changed it is quite unreal to me. My work has been busier than ever and I have had to sacrifice much of my personal project time to work and family. That isn't a bad thing by any means, but I do want to keep making progress on some of my personal AI projects.

AI has come so far since the last time I wrote an article.

  1. Anthropic’s Claude Mythos changed the AI security conversation
    1. Anthropic released “Claude Mythos Preview,” a frontier model focused on autonomous vulnerability discovery and exploit generation.
    2. Mythos reportedly found zero-days in major operating systems, browsers, OpenBSD, FFmpeg, and Linux subsystems.
  2. Linux Foundation Open Source and the Future of AI
    1. Profound statements on the use of AI and how it is evolving Software Engineering into architecture and design overseeing agentic implementation
    2. Torvalds allows AI contributions to the Linux kernel - Humans are responsible for the consequences, though!
  3. Robots are having their ChatGPT moment Boston Dynamics and friends are using NVIDIA robotics stack to create AI driven robots
  4. AI benchmarks are becoming solved
    1. Near-100% SWE-bench coding performance
    2. IMO gold-medal math performance (technically 2025)
    3. Google, Meta, and others are even more aggressively chasing Agentic AI (yes, Meta restructured again...)

There has been so many changes this year so far it is truly a sight to behold. We're on the cusp of the exponential curve of AI advancement in my opinion. I liken the state we're currently in to when the C compiler was developed and expanded the capability of writing programs to more people by abstracting away the complexities of Assembly.

We're here now, at the beginning, the first compiler after Assembly. The jump is starting to accelerate and I doubt that before long we'll have AI improving AI, which will be the next true marker of acceleration!

What I'm Excited About

I've been doing a lot of local experimentation with all sorts of AI tools. Everything from NotebookLM to study new concepts, to using Claude Code and Cursor to develop at the speed of my voice, to compiling ComfyUI graphs into image renderings, to building my own agent harness in the Zergling project. There is so much going on right now in the AI space that I'm relinquishing much of my skepticism. AI is here to stay. It has become a profound tool and, when wielded by those who are capable, can accomplish much in very short time.

It is the age of the generalist! With a "team of experts" in the palm of your hand, the true power lies in those with enough general knowledge to connect all of these experts. To stitch them into the fabric of creativity and bring forth their ideations with little effort of their own.

One of my favorite AI processes is to do a bit of design work up front, which usually involves chatting with AI, having the robot do some research and build up an archive of Obsidian markdown notes for me to peruse later. Then distilling those notes with my LLM companion into the concepts I need to know. Once we understand what we're doing, we set the robot down the path of implementing the prototype engine!

This involves "steering" the LLM. I don't say "create me an app that does X"...no, no. Instead, I like to design the modular layout of the software as if I were beginning to write it. "Build this REST API module here, expose routes A, B, and C" and "use this database that we decided on from our research, host it locally, define a schema and then let's review that schema, recall the schema must represent business models X, Y, and Z" and so on it goes. This way I have an understanding of the application I'm building while the LLM is building it. It helps me to debug the system and also to understand where points of debugging should be inserted.

In my own Zergling project, this first took form in the shape of a termianl REPL (read-evaluate-print-loop). When attempting to debug the inter-session communication between agents, I couldn't determine where the suspend and awake messages were hung up. I realized we need a way to inspect the message queues, chat sessions, actions, and tool calls of the agent teams and so instructed the LLM to build a REPL that interacts with a backend API. This allows us to do terminal-level testing, but also gives the LLM the ability to use terminal inputs in the REPL to examine the state of agents and debug itself! It's truly amazing.

Signing off

This is my first post in a while and I promise I will try hard to produce content each week this year. I have a very, very long list of topics I'd like to cover. On top of that there are several subjects I want to learn this year and I'm going to reproduce my learnings in blog posts like these or...who knows...maybe even some YouTube videos if I can convince myself to set it up!

Stay tuned for projects in the future!

For now it's back to work on the Zergling project and my new side project: ATLAS!! Details to come soon.

items.content_html:
Terminal Interpreter: Stop Googling awk

Terminal Interpreter Repo

Look, I've been writing shell commands for a long time. I still cannot remember the argument order for tar. I still can't remember whether it's find . -name or find -name ., or how to use awk to print a column from some output. Every time I want to xargs something I end up at man xargs to figure out -I {} vs -n 1 vs -J % or whatever it is.

I built a tool that lets me type the sentence instead.

Terminal Interpreter is a small Rust binary plus a thin shell widget. You type what you want at the prompt — "list files including hidden, sorted by mtime" — press Ctrl-G, and the current line is replaced in place with ls -alt. Press Enter to run, or edit first if the model got cute.

That's the whole pitch. One binary. One config file. A shell snippet that binds a hotkey. It's not an agent, it's not a chatbot, it's not trying to take over your terminal it's an LLM-flavoured autocompletereplace that runs exactly when you ask it to and then gets out of the way.

Let's dive into why I built this tool to help me protect myself from how stupid I am.

Starting From Zero

I need something to fix my commands when I type something like the following:

$ kubectl get wait shit how do i list all the containers in all pods

I knew exactly two things:

  1. I wanted a Rust binary. It's fast. It's cool. You love it. I love it.
  2. I wanted the LLM provider to be swappable. I bounce between OpenAI, Anthropic, and a local Ollama box depending on mood and what's running. It also helps you avoid tokenmaxxing and breaking the bank.
  3. I want it to be simpleAF to use.

Oh, that's three things.

This seems like a simple thing...and "why don't you just want to use an LLM prompt?" is the first question that comes to mind. Let's be honest, when you're working at the terminal you don't want to move to a prompt, or enter into a chat mode that you're talking back and forth with the model. The motivating part of this program to me is that interaction is easy. It's just ^G at the command line!

Rust? Yes, Rust.

I love Rust. It's a great language and getting better all the time. This is coming from an old C++/C developer, too! However, the models of the past (like 2024) were not so good at writing Rust code. Guess what? The models of the future (2026) are seemingly not-terrible at it! So, I decided to build a simple project in Rust this time. A reminder that I am a "directing prompter" -- I don't say things like "build me an application that does X" instead I say things like

Build me a Rust application using the clap library that initally supports only a config flag that points to a file that contains a json configuration file to configure our application and then all positional arguments as in interpreter -- the rest of the arguments. Those latter arguments will be the prompt that we will send to the LLM model. Use the llm crate for abstracting model choice and communication.

Then later, when I realized that I wanted to make installation and configuration as simple as I possibly can (and also experiment with a TUI application) I used a prompt like this

Great! Now we need to build a configuration TUI using the cursive library to configure our json object. Store this file in the local configuration directory for the OS (mac or linux) and build a hierarchical TUI using a dark mode with a slate blue tone that allows us to edit the JSON object. Inputs should accept a value when enter is pushed and cancel when escape is pushed. I want vim navigation alongside natural arrow keys. The TUI should populate with the values from the configuration file. Produce a model list for suggesting model choices and query for both the model and cost so users can make an informed decision about which model they want to use. The model selection menu should be a filterable list that uses enter to select a model. This should all be developed in a new module init_tui and use the cursive library to build it.

Not too explicitly descriptive, but also I'm telling this thing what I want and even sometimes where I want it. The downside is that I didn't get to learn myself how to use the cursive library. However, the upside is that this TUI was complete on the inside of a few minutes. I was off, I could interpreter --init and configure this application entirely!

Installation

From here, the natural point for me was to make the easiest possible installation and while I'm at it make a debuggable application while I'm building so I don't have to overwrite my installation of this binary once I get to the point of publishing it...which should be soon I hope.

I asked the robot to create interpreter --install which would create the configuration directory, put a sample json configuration file there with defaults, then run --init the TUI to configure the application. After changing any settings the user desires, the final output is how to install the zsh/fish/bash commands that set the bindkey and do the command line replacement.

The Shell Widget Is The Whole Product

What makes this tool feel like a tool is the shell integration: the moment where your half-baked command-with-words-in-it becomes a real command without you ever leaving the prompt.

In zsh, the entire integration is twenty lines:

_interpreter_widget() {
  emulate -L zsh
  local input=$BUFFER
  zle -R "interpreter: thinking…"

  local output rc
  output=$("$INTERPRETER_BIN" --config "$INTERPRETER_CONFIG" -- "$input" 2>/dev/null)
  rc=$?
  if (( rc != 0 )); then
    zle reset-prompt
    zle -M "interpreter: failed (exit $rc)"
    return 1
  fi

  BUFFER=${output%$'\n'}
  CURSOR=${#BUFFER}
  zle reset-prompt
}
zle -N _interpreter_widget
bindkey "$INTERPRETER_KEY" _interpreter_widget

This is the beauty is that it essentially overwrites your prompt with output from the model. Many of the other chat-in-terminal tools I've used are cumbersome to interact with. One of my favorite ones AIChat is much more powerful than this tool...but was too cumbersome to use to do the most useful part of it to me!

I just want to type some shit and get a command out. Better yet, use the history of commands in this shell and of things I've used over time to make an educated guess at what I'm trying to type.

It's handy. You should try it.

Some Context

We, avid users of LLMs, all know that Context Is King! The next feature I added was the ability to specify an additional context system prompt addition which is a standard way to add your own flavor to the model output.

The other thing I added though, after asking a few of my friends to test the CLI, was a "context files" feature that loads up file paths. These files are loaded into the context window of the model and can be used to add specialized information to the LLM such as commands you particularly use. Unforunately today this is single-command, so everything would have to be on one line to currently function, but perhaps in the future we can build a series of commands or something if that's necessary. For now, though, this is primarily a "hotfix my command" style interpreter.

Honestly, it really reminds me of fuck!

Loading these files has given me a real taste for the fine-tuning of LLM behavior though. Despite having a simple system prompt that directs models to prefer content from user-provided files....it often does not. It will take some tinkering to get this to work properly, but I see a bright future on the horizon.

Close

So far the little tool has proven to be quite useful to me. Especially in the age of context switching like mad between orchestration of agents in multiple projects...It was fun to use claude and vim in combination to edit a live codebase simultaneously using Rust. Even if it is a simpler project compared to others.

Oh, one other thing I forgot to mention. I built in Ollama support so you can run local models and not chew through 💰! You're welcome!

items.content_html:
I'm Sorry

First, let me apologize. I wrote this article post-mortem. I started implementing this next amount of work with the aim of doing my usual post as I went. Alas, I was completely carried away by the amount of progress I was making and I used the full extent of my wait-time to study the existing code to ensure I wasn't "coding myself into a box."

Wait-time: The time I spend waiting for AI to implement the next prompt.

I typically spend this time writing a blog post to catch you readers up on what I'm doing and my process, but this most recent time...I used it to study. This is one of the serious powers of vibe-coding-done-right, to me. If you can wield AI in specific, non-critical locations, then you can do additional work in the down time.

The changes that I've made to our application astound me. I have now realized there is a critical component that I needed to operate my network of agents in the fashion I want. I will talk about this more in a while...for now, I'm wondering if there's a potentially useful product in here somewhere -- if it doesn't exist already.

Let me say that I still have not fired up all of the new hotness: OpenClaw. In developing my system, and seeing videos and articles on OpenClaw, I wonder how similar my system may be to that one. However, I decided to carry on developing my own network for two main reasons. One: it's educational and I'm learning a ton about manipulating LLMs, and two: in the case that some of this technology does not exist, it will be very worthwhile to create (imo).

The Details

  • Massive UI changes
  • Introduction of agents
  • Streaming chat + my first debugging case (streaming tool calls)
  • Databases and Sessions
  • Background Agent to User communication
  • Software Development with AI in-mind: Building to Introspect (and Debug)
  • Iterative and Guided Development with AI
  • A Massive Prompt

Massive Prompt Time

Okay. Check this the fuck out. This is definitely my largest prompt to date and I think well-describes my paradigm of "Guided Development." I don't want to tell the AI at an extremely high level what I want and hope it gets the system right and constructed in an easily modifiable way. Instead, I want to help build a software architecture that is easy for me to reason about as well; maintaining one of my favorite software design principles: modularity.

We need to empower agent teams. I realized something quite interesting. we built queues for an agent for communication, but we did not build a way for agents to retain their session. This can matter if there is a call and response requirement such as when there is a user notification that requires a response. Let's create the agent_sessions table. This table will have rows identified by the agent ID. Similarly to the sessions tables, we'll track the agent session messages in an additional table. This will allow agents to build a long-running session of turns, tool calls, and communications -- or to shut down and be recalled retaining the full context of their prior execution. It will also help when agents communicate with one another to retain the context of that communication over time.

With this in place, I think we need to modify how agents and teams work. I thought we would be creating individual agents, but I see now that this is not the way forward. Instead, teams will be configured as a template for creating agents. We already have a couple of these configuration pieces in place: the system prompt and tool selection. We need additionally the ability to assign a model to the team, and the number of agents within the team. Now we can modify how teams behave! We will remove the agent messaging and work queues and instead we will have the Team messaging queue. The agent listener loop will be responsible for delegating messages to a new module, the agent_team_listener. This module will be responsible for holding the team queue and processing messages designated for a team. Each Team should spawn a new thread to perform its own listening loops so we can operate concurrently. This means that the agent_listener could likely become the agent_team_listener since the functionality will be quite similar, only instead of processing N queues, it will process only its own queue. We will

The team listener wil have agent-based responsibilities. It will create an agent if there is a slot available (recall teams have a specified number of agents), and will otherwise enqueue the work into a holding queue for when an agent completes their prior work. Agents within a team will have a lifecycle. If the requested agent does not yet exist, it will be created and the creation event (or hook) will take place. When an agent requests some work from another team or a response from the user, it will go into a suspended state. After the agent has completed the work assigned to it, it will go into the shutdown state. Let's talk about these states.
  • Creation: create the agent with corresponding new ID. Create the agent's session. Initiate the agent with the team's system prompt, toolbox, and model configuration. Initiate the chat session with the work message and details
  • Suspended: when awaiting feedback or output from another entity we suspend the agent. This is much like an async/await system. The agent will use a new tool, agentsuspend_with_message, which will take a message and suspend the agent until a response is reached. The message should generate a unique response ID (such as a UUID) and the team_listener will save the details of the agent and the response identifier along with any other details into a new suspended agents map (response ID -> agent). Suspended agents _should not be used to do new work. Each work item that requires new context will create a new agent. Suspended agents are woken when a message enters the team queue with the correct response ID, at which point the team listener waits for an open agent slot and revives the suspended agent, appends the response to its session, and re-initiates the chat loop.
  • Shutdown: At the completion of an agent's work item, that agent is shutdown. This means that all details are saved into the database, as they should be already due to agent session tracking, we can mark the agent session as complete in the database. Any resources in use are returned into the agent pool and this will free up an agent slot for use for future work.
Messages that go into team queues or the user notifications will have more details associated with them. All message originators need a unique message ID, this can be in the constructor for the message object itself. Messages should contain an originator data object that defines who the message came from, for example the user, another agent/team, or an event integration. This message ID can serve as the response ID in agents that require a response. Future messages that occur in response to another message, such as a user responding to a user notification, should have an originator_message_id parameter in the message that will capture the original message's ID (the response ID which will identify which agent needs to be woken up). With this in place, we should be able to facilitate back and forth messaging between entities. Note: event integration messages do not exist yet, it is just another example of a message type that will come in the future.

With all of these changes complete, we then need to modify the UI to suppor the new teams architecture. Creating a team will now require specifying a model and parameters for that model (context window, max tokens, etc.), the system prompt, the toolbox, and anything else. These should all be modifable properties as well, so we can change existing teams' settings. Responding to user notifications will require the additional message details to route messages to the correct team for waking up the suspended agent.

And the Plan that was generated:

Massive Agent Teams Refactor Plan

Agent sessions and teams architecture

Current state (brief)

  • DB: db.py: teams (name, system_prompt, tool_names), agents (name, team_id, model_id, max_context_tokens), sessions / session_messages (user chat), user_notifications, processed_queue_messages / agent_conversation_turns (one-shot work log). No agent-level session.
  • Queues: One team:{name}:work and one team:{name}:messaging per team; AgentListener polls all team work queues with a global semaphore, processes one message per worker, uses team config from first agent via get_team_agent_config.
  • Agents: Created explicitly via POST /agents (name, team_name, model_id?, context_window?); config split between team (system_prompt, tool_names) and agent (model, context).
  • Messages: Work payloads are free-form (e.g. {content}); no message_id or originator. submit_response only updates DB; no queue wake-up.

1. Database: agent sessions and schema shifts

New tables in
  • agent_sessions: id, agent_id (UNIQUE), created_at, updated_at, completed_at (NULL = active). One row per agent instance; keyed by agent id for long-running context.
  • agent_session_messages: id, agent_session_id, turn_index, originator, category, content, created_at. Same shape as session_messages for consistency; supports user, assistant, tool_call, tool_result, etc.
Team-as-template
  • teams: Add model_id, max_context_tokens, max_tokens (optional), num_agents (integer, default 1). Remove or stop using per-agent model/context; team holds model config.
  • agents: Becomes “pool members” created on demand: keep id, team_id; add name as optional/display (e.g. agent-{uuid}) or keep for compatibility; drop or ignore model_id / max_context_tokens on agent (read from team). No longer create agents by user-supplied name via API; creation is internal when team listener allocates a slot.
  • user_notifications: Add response_id (TEXT, UUID) and team_id (or team_name) for routing. When a notification is created from agent_suspend_with_message, set these so that on PATCH response we can enqueue to the correct team queue with originator_message_id = response_id.
Data layer
  • New module e.g. data/agent_sessions.py: create_agent_session(agent_id), get_agent_session(agent_id), append_agent_session_messages(session_id, messages), list_agent_session_messages(session_id, ...), complete_agent_session(agent_id) (set completed_at). Mirror patterns from data/sessions.py.
  • teams_agents.py: Add team columns and update_team_config(..., model_id=, max_context_tokens=, max_tokens=, num_agents=). Add create_agent_for_team(team_id) (insert agent, return agent_id) and get_team_config(team_id) returning full template (system_prompt, tool_names, model_id, max_context_tokens, max_tokens, num_agents). Deprecate or remove “get config from first agent” in favor of team-only config.
  • user_notifications.py: Add response_id, team_id to create_notification (optional); in submit_response, return or expose response_id and team_id so callers can enqueue the reply (or perform enqueue inside a new helper used by the route).

2. Message envelope and queue semantics

Unified message shape (for team queue and user-facing APIs)
  • message_id: Unique (e.g. UUID), set in constructor/factory for every message.
  • originator: Object describing source — e.g. { type: "user" }, { type: "agent", agent_id, team_id }, { type: "team", team_name }, { type: "event_integration", ... } for future use.
  • originator_message_id: Optional; when present, this message is a response to that message (e.g. user reply to a notification). Team listener uses this to wake the suspended agent that owns that response_id.
Concrete types
  • Extend or replace WorkItem (or introduce a TeamQueueMessage): required message_id, originator, optional originator_message_id, plus body (e.g. content, or structured payload). Keep backward compatibility for enqueue if needed (e.g. wrap legacy {content} in default originator).
  • InterTeamMessage: Add message_id, originator, originator_message_id for consistency when used in cross-team or wake-up flows.
Team queue only
  • Single queue per team: team:{team_name}:work. Remove per-agent work queues. Remove or repurpose team:{team_name}:messaging as needed (e.g. for inter-team or internal use); plan assumes work and wake-up both go to the team work queue with envelope fields.

3. Agent lifecycle and team listener

States
  • Creation: Team listener allocates an agent (insert row), creates agent_session, builds initial chat from team config (system_prompt, tool_names, model_id, max_context_tokens, max_tokens) and work message, runs chat loop.
  • Suspended: Agent calls agent_suspend_with_message; server generates response_id, stores in suspended_agents[response_id] = (agent_id, session_id, …), creates user notification with response_id and team_id. Chat loop stops until a message with originator_message_id == response_id arrives.
  • Shutdown: After agent “finishes” (no suspend, no more tool rounds), mark agent_session complete, release slot, optionally mark agent row for reuse or leave as-is.
agent_team_listener (new module, e.g. )
  • Holds: team_id / team_name, team queue (single), num_agents (from team config), in-memory slot pool (e.g. semaphore or set of “busy” agent ids), suspended_agents dict: response_id -> { agent_id, session_id, ... }, and a holding queue (in-memory deque or list) for messages that arrived when no slot was free.

  • Loop (single thread/task per team):

    1. Wait for message on team queue (or take from holding queue when a slot frees).
    2. If message has originator_message_id and it’s in suspended_agents: pop entry, load agent session, append response as user message, re-run chat loop for that agent (same run_chat, tools include agent_suspend_with_message and notify), then on next shutdown release slot and optionally push next holding item to queue.
    3. Else if message is “new” work and there’s a free slot: create agent + agent_session, init chat with work message, run chat (may suspend or shutdown); if shutdown, release slot and process holding.
    4. Else: push message to holding queue.
  • Run chat: same pattern as current listener (build ChatRequest from session history, call run_chat, handle tool_uses; on agent_suspend_with_message tool call, generate response_id, register in suspended_agents, create notification, return from run so the loop “stops” for that agent until wake).
agent_suspend_with_message tool (mcp_server.py or MCP layer)
  • Params: e.g. message, title, summary (similar to notify). Generates response_id (UUID), creates user notification with response_requested=True, response_id, team_id. Returns { response_id, notification_id, ... }. The team listener must intercept this tool result: when the model calls this tool, the runner (inside team listener) does not append a normal tool result and continue; it registers the agent in suspended_agents and exits the chat loop for that agent. So the tool can be implemented as a special case in the team listener’s tool-dispatch (or the MCP tool returns a sentinel that the listener treats as “suspend and store response_id”).
agent_listener refactor (main.py + agent/agent_listener.py)
  • agent_listener becomes a thin coordinator: on startup, for each team (from DB), ensure team:{name}:work queue exists, create one AgentTeamListener instance, start one asyncio task per team running that instance’s run_loop(). No longer register multiple queues on a single global loop; each team task runs independently.
  • Remove global “register team work queue” from data_routes.py for new model: new teams get a queue when the team is created; POST to enqueue still creates queue if missing and ensures a team listener is attached (or team listeners are created at startup from DB only).

4. User notification response → wake suspended agent

  • When user submits response: PATCH /notifications/{id}/response with response_text. Backend: submit_response updates notification; then (in route or a small service) fetch notification row for response_id and team_id, enqueue to team:{team_name}:work a message with message_id=new_uuid, originator={ type: "user" }, originator_message_id=response_id, content=response_text. Team listener will dequeue, match originator_message_id to suspended_agents, wake agent, append message, resume chat.

5. API and routes

  • Teams: POST /agents/teams (or similar) to create team with name, model_id, max_context_tokens, max_tokens, num_agents, system_prompt, tool_names. PATCH /agents/teams/{id} extended to allow updating model_id, max_context_tokens, max_tokens, num_agents, system_prompt, tool_names. GET /agents continues to list teams with config (and can list “current” pool agents per team if desired).
  • Agents: Remove or deprecate POST /agents (create agent by name). Agents are created internally by team listener. Optional: GET /agents/teams/{id}/agents to list active/pool agents for a team.
  • Queues: POST /data/queues/{queue_name}/messages body must support new envelope: message_id, originator, originator_message_id, plus payload. If omitted, generate default message_id and originator for backward compatibility.
  • Notifications: PATCH /notifications/{id}/response body unchanged; server-side uses stored response_id and team_id to enqueue the reply to the correct team queue.

6. UI (zergling-web-ui)

  • Teams (Agents page): Create team with required fields: name, model (dropdown from /models), context window, max tokens, num_agents, system prompt, toolbox. Edit team: same fields editable. Remove “Create agent” (name + team); show teams and their config plus optional “pool size” or “active agents” read-only.
  • Notifications: When submitting a response, no change to the payload; backend uses response_id + team_id to route. If the UI ever needs to show “which team this notification belongs to”, add optional display from API (e.g. notification detail includes team_name or response_id for debugging).

7. Documentation and tests

  • Update CONTEXT.md and architecture per RULES.md: agent_sessions, team-as-template, one queue per team, agent_team_listener, lifecycle, message envelope, notification response routing.
  • Tests: unit tests for agent_sessions CRUD, team config (create/update with model and num_agents), message envelope parsing, team listener behavior (allocate slot, suspend, wake, shutdown); integration test for enqueue response with originator_message_id and wake-up.

Implementation order (suggested)

  1. DB schema: agent_sessions, agent_session_messages; team columns (model_id, max_context_tokens, max_tokens, num_agents); user_notifications.response_id, team_id. Data layer for agent_sessions and team config.
  2. Message envelope: WorkItem/TeamQueueMessage with message_id, originator, originator_message_id; adopt in queue and APIs.
  3. agent_suspend_with_message tool and notification creation with response_id + team_id; submit_response enqueue-to-team logic.
  4. AgentTeamListener: single-queue loop, slot pool, suspended_agents, holding queue, create agent + session, run chat, handle suspend/shutdown.
  5. Refactor startup: one AgentTeamListener task per team; remove old AgentListener multi-queue loop.
  6. API: teams CRUD with full config; deprecate or remove POST create agent; queues accept new envelope.
  7. UI: team create/edit with model and params; remove create-agent flow; notifications response unchanged (backend routing).

Diagram (high level)

flowchart LR
  subgraph teams [Teams]
    T1[Team A]
    T2[Team B]
  end
  subgraph queues [Team queues]
    Q1["team🅰️work"]
    Q2["team🅱️work"]
  end
  subgraph listeners [Team listeners]
    L1[AgentTeamListener A]
    L2[AgentTeamListener B]
  end
  T1 --> Q1
  T2 --> Q2
  Q1 --> L1
  Q2 --> L2
  L1 --> Pool1[Slot pool A]
  L2 --> Pool2[Slot pool B]
  L1 --> Suspended1[suspended_agents A]
  L2 --> Suspended2[suspended_agents B]
  User[User / API] --> Q1
  User --> Q2
  Notif[Notification response] --> Q1
stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> Creation
  Creation --> Running
  Running --> Suspended : agent_suspend_with_message
  Running --> Shutdown : work complete
  Suspended --> Running : message with originator_message_id
  Shutdown --> [*]

This turned out to be a ridiculous success and I was able to properly orchestrate agents and allow them to communicate between one another. They could develop a request for work and submit that work to the appropriate agent team and then shut down and await a response from that team that work was completed, then we reload the context and pick up where we left off.

It was really quite amazing to see this work in action and I started with a very simple email reading team that would get emails and read their contents, then query for agent teams and determine which one was best able to handle the content in the message, such as scheduling events when my kids' schools emailed us with event changes and updates.

This is the foundation of the new reality of the Zergling project. In order to create a true agent swarm, they need to be able to communicate with each other and delegate tasks to each other. Over my time using agents, I've found that there is much more to gain by creating focused, specific agents that have a collection of skills and tools to do exactly what they need.

This is fundamentally Context Engineering!

I'm going to keep this article short and sweet as I begin to re-imagine my entire project.
items.title: The New Reality
items.summary: I re-developed a major portion of this application over a couple of days and I'm here to report the insane changes that have come to the system.

items.content_html:
Local Models

Today, we're getting started with local modeling. I ultimately would like to use Ollama, but I may start with LM Studio just to get off the ground quickly. Let me take a quick look at Ollama and what it would take to get a model running:

tell me about the Ollama server and API. What steps do I take to download the deepseek-coder-v2-lite-instruct model and run it as a server?

While it finds the answer, I will say that I do enjoy LM Studio and it is great for any beginner looking to get into local modeling. It makes searching for models that fit into your hardare a breeze. The studio takes care of CPU/GPU offloading of the model and even partial offloading for models that are too large to fit into your VRAM. If you don't know what any of that means, LM Studio is the choice for you!

Ollama server + API (what it is)

Ollama runs models locally and exposes an HTTP API on http://localhost:11434/api by default.

Core inference endpoints:
  • POST /api/generate (single-prompt completions).
  • POST /api/chat (chat-style messages; streaming by default, can disable).
Download DeepSeek-Coder-V2-Lite-Instruct

You have two common “names” you might mean:

Option A (official library entry)

Pick a tag from the deepseek-coder-v2 library that matches “16b-lite-instruct …”. For example:

deepseek-coder-v2:16b-lite-instruct-q4_0

Download:
ollama pull deepseek-coder-v2:16b-lite-instruct-q4_0

No details on offloading so I ask

does ollama handle gpu offloading and partial loading?
3) Automatic behavior

By default, Ollama:
  • Detects available GPU(s)
  • Estimates available VRAM
  • Offloads as many layers as will fit
  • Falls back to CPU if necessary
You do not need to manually split layers in typical usage.

Great, so I don't have to do anything, maybe we will give Ollama a try? One more question, though. How does quantization work in ollama? If you don't know what quantization is I recommend Hugging Face Docs on it. I'll explain it briefly and assume you have some technical chops. Fundamentally, we are using quanitzation to reduce the bit-storage of numbers. This means that we take a Float64 and represent it as a Float32 for 32-bit quantization. We can go even farther down in modeling to FP8 or FP4. Using so few bits to represent numbers can result in a loss of accuracy, however testing generally shows that 8-bit quantization results in a loss of only 5% or so. This can be somewhat difficult to verify so if you're testing your model and seeing issues, quantization could help recover some accuracy (increasing the number of bits, that is, not decreasing it). Looking at the docs it appears that we can pull several models, including pre-quantized models!

A note on quantization: there are a few formats of quantization. You'll see strings like Q5_0 or Q4_K_M. These tell us something about how these larger floating point numbers are quantized. K is for K-block quantization. This is generally the format you'll want to look for the _0 format is a standard quantization and can result in more error. K-block quantization uses a mathematical formula to pack information into sub-blocks of the bits. It uses a scaling factor s to quantize a given model weight and that weight is reconstructed during inference time by multiplying by the s factor. The trick in here is choosing s wisely, and there's a formula for that as well. The _M packages the K-block quantization using Mixed Precision, allowing lower bit counts for the weights themselves, but higher bit precision for the scaling factors. This allows us to pay a small size penalty in the form of a larger storage for s, yet retain floating point accuracy for the weights w that we store in the lower bit widths, such as a Q4_K_M which would use 4 bits for the weights, but allow for FP16 for the scale factor s. Recall s is used to 'rebuild' the original weight at inference time, so we pay an additional multiplication to reconstitute the weight by its scale.

When you think about it, this is really fucking cool technology that allows us plebians with paltry 12GB VRAM gpus to run some larger consumer models that have 'more intelligence' within them!

So, anyway, when I started writing this I was downloading a model in LM Studio to use...but now I think I may just use ollama since it's more "developer friendly." Let's get started!

Popping on over to the Ollama Library because there's no way to search for these models from the CLI, that I can tell anyway. Ah, okay. Was about to complain about the inability to search for any particular metadata about models, but then I found Ollama Search which allows me to at least specify tool usage. Agh. I still can't search by model size or anything though...that's a real bummer...

Time to search for some tools or make one. I immediately find Gollama which has a terminal interface for models. I guess let's give this a try...as long as it has vim-like navigation. I'm sure we'll do okay. I really need to turn these sessions into a live YouTube or something. There's a package for this in the Arch AUR, so let's get to building it!

This isn't a searching tool though...that would be a great addition to this, I wonder if I can find the time to add to this tool. Well, back to the search library then, if I can't find a good model in the next 5 minutes, I'm going back to LM Studio for the rest of this article!

Here's the list of models I'm going to pull:

  • glm-4.7-flash:q4_K_M - which I'll likely have issues running in my maesely 12GB VRAM
  • gpt-oss:20b - probably will be partially offloaded since there are no quantization options
  • qwen3-vl:8b - qwen but can operate computer interfaces!?
  • qwen3:14b - the standard qwen3 model that can fit entirely in my VRAM
  • deepseek-r1:14b - deepsink thinking mode model
  • ministral-3:14b - solid model that has native function calling and json output

I've never tried ministral, so I'll start with that one. While that gets downloading, let's add ollama server support to our interface server!

I need to take a look at the Ollama docs so I can craft this implementation prompt properly. Can we list all of the models we have downloaded? Can we switch which model is loaded? Can we get status information about this occurring? All things we want to include in our server if possible. There is an ollama library...so perhaps we'll use that instead of the web API? Although I do like keeping this thing rather agnostic in the form of maintaining HTTP-only communication.

Checking out the Streaming docs

Streaming is enabled by default through the REST API, but disabled by default in the SDKs.

Which is great, that means we can stream data to the frontend as we get it. If we add a communication layer like texting or discord or something in the future, we may need to control this, but for now this is what we want so we can use the chat UI. There is support for thinking as well, so we'll need to include that, too. I'm beginning to wonder if I should start implementing some of this instead of relying so heavily on AI to implement it, but that wouldn't be in the spirit of this blog...so let's see what we can make happen!

We're going to introduce ollama as a local server. This means we need to add support for a new provider, the local ollama provider. This provider does not require any API keys. We want to maintain a set of features: the ability to list the models installed locally and select one, we would like to support the streaming api so we can receive messages from the model as they are emit, as well we need to support thinking models as well which can show us the thinking trace. We will need special UI treatment for the thinking case, this should be rendered into an expandable container so we can see the thinking as it occurs, but then as the resulting messages are rendered, the thinking panel should collapse so we can see the messages.

This model is thinking its pants off! I admit I'm growing skeptical that I should implement this myself...the plan comes back, though -- and here it is:

Ollama provider, streaming, and thinking UI Plan

Ollama provider, streaming, and thinking UI

Current state

  • Providers: zergling-agent-server/providers/base.py defines ModelProvider with list_models(api_key) and chat(...) returning ChatRoundResult (content or tool_uses). Routing in get_provider_for_model(model_id) is by prefix: claude- → Anthropic, gpt-/o1-/o3- → OpenAI.
  • Chat: Single round or multi-round (tool loop) via POST /chat; no streaming. main.py requires an API key per provider in _get_api_key_for_provider and _get_api_key_for_list_models.
  • Web UI: zergling-web-ui/src/lib/agent.ts has getModels() (flattens all vendors) and postChat() (returns { content }). chat/+page.svelte requires API key when !key && !config?.has_api_key, then appends a single assistant message with res.content. No streaming, no thinking.

Architecture (high level)

sequenceDiagram
  participant UI as Web_UI
  participant Server as Agent_Server
  participant Ollama as Ollama_Local

  UI->>Server: GET /models
  Server->>Ollama: GET /api/tags
  Ollama-->>Server: model list
  Server-->>UI: ollama: { models: [{ id: "ollama/llama3" }] }

  UI->>Server: POST /chat/stream (model: ollama/llama3)
  Server->>Ollama: POST /api/chat stream=true think=true
  loop NDJSON
    Ollama-->>Server: message.thinking / message.content
    Server-->>UI: SSE thinking / content
  end
  Server-->>UI: SSE done
  • Model IDs: Ollama provider will expose models with IDs like ollama/ (e.g. ollama/llama3, ollama/qwen3) so routing is unambiguous and the server can pass to the Ollama API.
  • Ollama base URL: Default http://localhost:11434; override via env ZERGLING_OLLAMA_BASE_URL (server-side).

1. Ollama provider (no API key, list models, non-streaming chat)

  • New file: zergling-agent-server/providers/ollama.py

    • vendor_id = "ollama".
    • list_models(api_key): Ignore API key. GET {base_url}/api/tags. Map each model’s name to { "id": "ollama/" + name }. Return ListModelsResult(models=..., error=...) on failure (e.g. connection refused).
    • chat(...): POST {base_url}/api/chat with stream: false, messages (convert from canonical format: system as first message, user/assistant as-is; Ollama supports system/user/assistant). Optionally set think: true for thinking models. Map response message.content and message.thinking (if present) into a single text for this round if desired, or ignore thinking for non-streaming; message.tool_calls → ToolUse list. Use num_predict for max_tokens.
    • to_tools(canonical_tools): Map to Ollama tool format (type: "function", function.name, function.description, function.parameters).
  • Registry and routing: In providers/.py register OllamaProvider(). In providers/base.py, extend get_provider_for_model(model_id) so that if model_id.strip().lower().startswith("ollama/") return PROVIDER_REGISTRY.get("ollama").
  • API key: In main.py: in _get_api_key_for_list_models, for vendor_id == "ollama" return None. In _get_api_key_for_provider, for vendor_id == "ollama" return "" (no key required). Optionally extend GET /config with has_ollama (e.g. true if Ollama base URL is reachable); not strictly required for “no API key when using Ollama” (UI can infer from model id).

2. Streaming API (server)

  • Provider interface: Add an optional streaming capability. In providers/base.py introduce a small StreamEvent type (e.g. {"type": "thinking", "delta": str} | {"type": "content", "delta": str} | {"type": "done", "content": str | None, "tool_uses": list | None}) and an optional method stream_chat(self, ...) that returns an async iterator of StreamEvent. Default: not implemented (base can raise or we check with hasattr(provider, "stream_chat")).
  • Ollama streaming: In ollama.py, implement stream_chat: POST Ollama /api/chat with stream: true, think: true. Read NDJSON line-by-line; for each chunk emit thinking deltas from message.thinking and content deltas from message.content; on done: true emit done with full accumulated content and optional tool_uses (if any). Handle tool_calls in stream by accumulating and passing through in done.
  • New endpoint: In main.py add POST /chat/stream (same body/headers as /chat). If the resolved provider does not support stream_chat, return 400 or 501. Otherwise, run a single round: call stream_chat, return a StreamingResponse with text/event-stream, sending SSE events for each StreamEvent (e.g. event: thinking, data: {"delta":"..."}; event: content, data: {"delta":"..."}; event: done, data: {"content":"...", "tool_uses": null}). Do not run the tool loop inside the stream; if done includes tool_uses, client can continue with POST /chat and the same conversation.

3. Web UI: model list and API key

  • Models: No change needed; GET /models already aggregates vendors, so Ollama models (e.g. ollama/llama3) will appear in the dropdown.
  • API key when using Ollama: In zergling-web-ui/src/routes/chat/+page.svelte, require API key only when the selected model is not an Ollama model. Use a predicate like model.startsWith("ollama/") so that for ollama/* we allow sending without key (and without config.has_api_key). Optionally in zergling-web-ui/src/routes/+layout.svelte adjust the API key placeholder when an Ollama model is selected (e.g. “Not needed for Ollama”).

4. Web UI: streaming client and thinking UI

  • Streaming client: In zergling-web-ui/src/lib/agent.ts add postChatStream(opts: ChatOptions): Promise> (or an async generator / callback-based API). It will fetch POST /chat/stream with body/headers same as postChat, then read the response body as SSE and yield { type: 'thinking', delta }, { type: 'content', delta }, { type: 'done', content, toolUses? }. Only use for models that support streaming (e.g. model.startsWith("ollama/")); otherwise fall back to postChat.
  • Chat page state: Extend assistant message shape to optional thinking?: string so we can store both thinking and content (e.g. { role: 'assistant', content: string, thinking?: string }).
  • Send flow: When the selected model is Ollama, call postChatStream instead of postChat. Create a new assistant message with content: '' and thinking: ''; then for each event append to thinking or content, and on done set final content (and optionally handle toolUses by showing a message or switching to non-streaming follow-up).

  • Thinking UI: For each assistant message that has thinking:

    • Render an expandable section (e.g. a
      or a button + collapsible div) labeled “Thinking”.
    • While streaming and thinking is being updated, keep the thinking section expanded and update its body in real time.
    • When the first content delta arrives (or when stream ends), collapse the thinking section so the main message content is visible; the thinking block remains available when expanded.
  • Markdown: Keep using MarkdownRenderer for the main content; render thinking as plain text (or simple pre-wrap) inside the expandable.

5. Documentation and config

  • Config: Extend GET /config in main.py with has_ollama: bool (e.g. by doing a quick GET to {OLLAMA_BASE_URL}/api/tags at startup or on request, or always true if we don’t want a dependency; optional).
  • Architecture docs: Update architecture/zergling-agent-server/model-providers.md with Ollama (vendor_id, model prefix ollama/, no API key, list via /api/tags, chat/stream via /api/chat). Update architecture/zergling-web-ui/chat-and-tools.md (or app-structure) to mention streaming and thinking UI for Ollama.

File summary

Tool use and streaming (scope)

  • First version: Streaming is single-round. If Ollama returns tool_calls in the stream, include them in the done event; the client can show that tools were requested and either call POST /chat with the conversation + tool results to continue, or show a short message. Full multi-round tool execution inside the same stream can be a later enhancement.
  • Non-streaming POST /chat with Ollama will still support the existing tool loop (Ollama’s chat with stream: false can return tool_calls; we already have tool execution in main.py for other providers).

A couple of strong details I like to see:

Ollama provider (no API key, list models, non-streaming chat)

We're implementing non-streaming chat despite asking for streaming. This is a good thing. We want to maintain existing functionality and support for non-streaming models. It's also easier to debug and fits within our existing framework.

After we get non-streaming online, then we can look at more complex behaviors and start to add those in.

Provider interface: Add an optional streaming capability

Optional. Great call. Using a StreamEvent type which defines some of the types that we see emit from models including thinking, content | delta and tool_uses that come back from the model. Ultimately, in our UI, we want to render all of these, especially tool calls, so we can debug what the model is doing and have full awareness. This will be the staging ground for allowing "signoffs" on tool calls to prevent misuse.

Async generators

For both Python and the web UI, we're using streams. Async generators in Python and ReadableStream in the UI. I hope both of these will work as well as I've used them in the past! I'm excited to see if this AI can manage to successfully implement streaming and thinking from these models. After we get streaming represented properly in the Web UI, I will continue to prompt the implementation of tool calls and imbue our server with the ability to describe succinctly to users what tools are doing and perhaps some iconography as well so they will look nice in the stream of data we receive from models.

Our application is really taking shape!

Architecture docs

It calls out updating the architecture docs. Yes!! Honestly, these updates suggested look light, but hopefully they will end up being decent. I have been debating if it's better to have code-level documentation or markdown-level documentation for AI projects. Code-level docs are obviously better for us humans who bounce around the code, but for context-kings like AI models...I wonder if really our markdown documentation should be the prominent area for "loading up information" about our codebase in the form of context engineering.

Architecture docs: Update architecture/zergling-agent-server/model-providers.md with Ollama (vendor_id, model prefix ollama/, no API key, list via /api/tags, chat/stream via /api/chat). Update architecture/zergling-web-ui/chat-and-tools.md (or app-structure) to mention streaming and thinking UI for Ollama.

The Build

Alright, the build of the plan is complete and I load it up and what happens?

RuntimeError: Ollama API error: {"error":"\"ministral-3:14b\" does not support thinking"}

We need to give this server the capability to understand what features models even support. Is that possible in Ollama? It is.

examine https://docs.ollama.com/api-reference/show-model-details and query for model capabilities. We do not want to allow thinking on models that do not support that capability.

However...we get another error when using deepseek:

RuntimeError: Ollama API error: {"error":"registry.ollama.ai/library/deepseek-r1:14b does not support tools"}

Back to the prompting board.

okay, now we also need to check the capabilities list for "tools" to support tool calls, otherwise the model will fail

Homie added a capabilities cache!

    async def _model_capabilities(self, ollama_model: str) -> list[str]:
        global _capabilities_cache
        if ollama_model in _capabilities_cache:
            return _capabilities_cache[ollama_model]
        try:
            async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=10.0) as client:
                response = await client.post(
                    f"{self._base()}/api/show",
                    json={"model": ollama_model},
                )
        except httpx.RequestError:
            _capabilities_cache[ollama_model] = []
            return []
        if response.status_code != 200:
            _capabilities_cache[ollama_model] = []
            return []
        data = response.json()
        caps = data.get("capabilities")
        if not isinstance(caps, list):
            caps = []
        capabilities = [c for c in caps if isinstance(c, str)]
        _capabilities_cache[ollama_model] = capabilities
        return capabilities

I guess that's cool, but that means I now need to restart the server each time we add one of these capability changes. Also, what the fuck is this capabilities = [c for c in caps if isinstance(c, str)]!? Is that really necessary?

Oh shit, I have to record this one to see it.

My system prompt kind of messed it up, but we'll get this live in the next blog where we add more to our system to configure system prompts, tools, and skills! For now, this is awesome!!!! We have local LLMs running!
items.title: Local Models And Something Useful
items.summary: Connecting our server to a local LLM and then building something useful out of it by connecting it to my gmail and google calendar and having it schedule events.

items.content_html:
Picking Up Where We Left Off

Oooh, title case, gotta love it.

What were we doing last time? Let's just ask:

what were we doing in our last commit and what do you think our next step should be if we want to introduce an abstraction on model vendor choice?

While it examines what we've done, I want to ask an interesting thing of the model. I want it to document what we have constructed inside of a markdown file. I want this file to describe our modules and implementation choices, interfaces, utilities, libraries, all of the goodies. With this over-arching description of our project, I can begin to manipulate the context of the model and point it to subsections of the project to work on. This will be very powerful (I suspect) for our current goal of running LOCAL LLMS! 💰

Let's look at that prompt.

I would like you to build a document hierarchy. Create a new top-level directory called architcture/ and in this directory I would like you to write markdown files describing our entire codebase. These documents should be relatively concise, describing only what they need to and when more detailed information is needed, link out to another markdown document. As an example, we have four main projects so far: zergling-extension, zergling-agent-server, zergling-web-ui, and zergling-voice2text. Let's order this folder hierarchically as well, with each directory containing a CONTENTS.md file. The CONTENTS.md file will be a brief description of the current level of the hierarchy and act as a table of contents for the sibling markdown files in the same directory. This means there will be four folders, one for each project, each containing a CONTENTS.md. As an example of what these directories contain, take zergling-agent-server: there should be a document describing the web server and the api and another document describing the mcp server and integrated tools. As you scan each project and compose this document hierarchy, group related information in the same document. Once you are done, generate a RULES.md file in the root of the repository that describes the rules for reading and modifying these documents.

WOOF! What a mouthful. Let's see if this works. This agent will churn in the background while we get back to work. This, to me, is some of the most power to gain from agents. I love modularity and I aim to instruct our agents to maintain my love with the ulterior goal of having discrete modules that encapsulate work...much like the paradigm itself! We know that if we have some module that loads files and that module has a public interface and is sound and well tested that we don't need to think about what that module does, only what it provides for us! We're going to exploit this to the optimus!

What the last commit did

The last commit introduced the Zergling agent server, REPL, and web UI, and then added MCP tool support across all of them.

  • Agent server (zergling-agent-server)

    • FastAPI app that proxies chat to the Anthropic Messages API.
    • POST /chat: forwards messages (and optional system/tools) to Anthropic; supports tool-use loop (up to 10 rounds).
    • GET /config, GET /health, GET /tools (from MCP), GET /models (vendor-keyed: anthropic from Anthropic’s API, openai as a placeholder). API key from env or x-zergling-api-key; model/endpoint from headers.
    • MCP app mounted at /mcp; tools (e.g. list_directory) are converted to Anthropic tool format and executed in the loop.

  • REPL

    • Adds /tool (list/set/clear tools), sends tool_names in chat when set, shows active tools in the banner.

  • Web UI

    • Tools row and expandable tools panel; activeToolNames in settings and sent with chat.
So the current state is: one backend (Anthropic only) for both listing models and performing chat; OpenAI is only a placeholder in the /models response.
Ah, shit. Bullets don't render in callouts. I wonder if the blog agent can fix that while I keep working... [prompt: I can't put bullets in my > > >

Also, we're making use of AGENTS.md. Because I want to be model agnostic, I don't want to use specifically cursor rules. We're going to have our local agents obey AGENTS.md as well! Here's all I'm putting in there right now:

# Role

You are an expert programmer. You write concise code and use modularity to
maintain a clean and simple codebase.

# Always

- See architecture/CONTENTS.md for documentation on the project.
  - Observe RULES.md when modifying architecture documentation
- Prefer unit tests. Do not create new files for one off testing. Write test
  suites.
- Run python in a virtualenv

Start simple. Add as needed. We'll refine this over time and I will point out whenever I do. Back to the next step, model modularity. Here's the paraphrased output of "what we should do next" from the AI point of view.

  • Define a small provider interface (e.g. in zergling-agent-server/):
  • Implement an Anthropic provider that wraps the current logic:
  • Route by model (or vendor) in the API layer:
  • Keep tool handling vendor-aware

Hmm, not bad. I'm going to ask it to write a plan for this, but to include in this plan developing an abstract class for vendors, or potentially models? -- so we can add new models as we use them. Let's get started.

Great idea. Develop a plan on the changes we plan to make. Consider using an abstract class to create a model agnostic interface. We know we'll want to chat with the models, and we know we'll need tools, be sure to include these features.

Have to give the guy some encouragement, he's doing a great job. While this thing churns I'll say that GPT-5 is great for this planning feature. I'm thinking that when we get our local model swarm off the ground, we may use a very "intelligent" model to do our planning and have it compose documents or tickets of what needs to be done, then pass that to an orchestrator who spins up implementing agents. I'll get a diagram for this as we get closer.

The plan is solid. I want to find a way to start including these plans in the blog posts in an expanding output or something so that you can read what I'm generating and have context when I make suggestions to the agent about the plan. Let me see if this blog agent can figure that out.............Alright, here's the plan after some back and forth while this collapsible section was built in the blog.

Model Vendor Abstraction Plan
name: Model vendor abstraction overview: Introduce an abstract ModelProvider base class in the agent server that defines a vendor-agnostic interface for listing models and performing chat (including tool-use rounds). Move the existing Anthropic logic behind an AnthropicProvider implementation and route requests in main.py via the abstraction. todos:
  • id: base-interface content: Add providers/base.py with ABC, result types, registry, get_provider_for_model status: completed
  • id: anthropic-provider content: Implement AnthropicProvider (list_models from API, chat, to_tools) status: completed
  • id: main-refactor content: Refactor main.py to use providers for /models and /chat; no static model lists status: completed
  • id: openai-provider content: Implement OpenAIProvider (list_models from OpenAI API, chat with tool_calls, to_tools); register and route gpt-* status: in_progress isProject: false

Model vendor abstraction plan

Goal

Support multiple model vendors (Anthropic now, OpenAI later) behind a single API by defining a model-agnostic interface and moving Anthropic behind it. Chat and tools are first-class: the interface includes listing models, sending chat with optional tools, and handling tool-use rounds.

Current state

  • main.py: All vendor logic is inline—Anthropic Messages API for GET /models (lines 124–188) and POST /chat (lines 219–307), including the tool-use loop and MCP tool execution.
  • Tools: MCP exposes tools as {name, description, input_schema}; Anthropic uses the same shape. The server converts by name, runs tools via mcp.get_tools() and tool.run(input), and appends tool_result blocks to messages.

Architecture

flowchart LR
    subgraph api [HTTP API]
        GET_models[GET /models]
        POST_chat[POST /chat]
    end
    subgraph routing [Routing]
        resolve[Resolve provider by model]
    end
    subgraph providers [Providers]
        ABC[ModelProvider ABC]
        Anthropic[AnthropicProvider]
        OpenAI[OpenAIProvider]
        ABC --> Anthropic
        ABC --> OpenAI
    end
    subgraph mcp [MCP]
        get_tools[get_tools_for_api]
        run_tool[tool.run]
    end
    GET_models --> resolve
    POST_chat --> resolve
    resolve --> Anthropic
    Anthropic --> get_tools
    POST_chat --> run_tool
  • One round per provider: Each provider implements a single chat round (request → response). The server owns the tool loop: it calls the provider; if the response is tool_use, it runs MCP tools, appends results to messages, and calls the provider again (up to N rounds).
  • Canonical tools: MCP gives a canonical list [{name, description, input_schema}]. Each provider converts that to its own API format and parses tool_use from its response into a normalized shape so the server can execute by name and feed back.

1. Abstract interface and types

New module: zergling-agent-server/providers/base.py (or a single providers.py if preferred).

  • Result types (vendor-agnostic):

    • ListModelsResult: models: list[dict] (each with at least id; optional display_name, created_at, type), optional error: str, optional pagination fields (has_more, first_id, last_id).
    • ChatRoundResult: either final text or tool uses—e.g. a small dataclass or TypedDict with content: str | None and tool_uses: list[ToolUse] | None where one is set; ToolUse has id, name, input: dict.

  • Abstract base class ModelProvider (use abc.ABC and @abstractmethod):

    • vendor_id: str — e.g. "anthropic" (class or instance attribute).
    • list_models(self, api_key: str | None) -> ListModelsResult — async; fetches model list for this vendor (or returns error if key missing/invalid).
    • chat(self, messages: list[dict], model: str, system: str, max_tokens: int, tools: list[dict] | None) -> ChatRoundResult — async; one round. tools are in vendor-specific format (provider converts from canonical internally or receives already converted; see below).
    • to_tools(self, canonical_tools: list[dict]) -> list[dict] — convert from MCP/canonical {name, description, input_schema} to the format this vendor’s API expects. For Anthropic the canonical shape is already compatible; for OpenAI this would map to function-calling format.
So the server will: get canonical tools from MCP; call provider.to_tools(canonical); in a loop call provider.chat(messages, model, system, max_tokens, tools); if result has content, return it; else for each tool_use run MCP by name, append assistant + user tool_result messages, repeat.

2. Anthropic provider

New module: zergling-agent-server/providers/anthropic.py.
  • Implement ModelProvider: vendor_id = "anthropic".
  • list_models: Move the existing GET logic from main.py (lines 131–186) into this method—httpx to ANTHROPIC_MODELS_URL with pagination, return ListModelsResult with models (and optional error, has_more, etc.).
  • to_tools: Accept canonical list; return as-is (Anthropic format matches) or ensure input_schema has type: "object"; same as current _anthropic_tools_from_names but keyed by the canonical list, not by names filter.

  • chat: Accept messages, model, system, max_tokens, tools. Build request, POST to DEFAULT_ENDPOINT (or configurable), use anthropic-version and x-api-key. Parse response:

    • If stop_reason == "end_turn": return ChatRoundResult(content=text).
    • If stop_reason == "tool_use": extract tool_use blocks to ToolUse(id, name, input) and return ChatRoundResult(tool_uses=[...]).
    • Otherwise raise or return an error result.
Dependencies (httpx) stay in the provider; no need to change requirements.txt if httpx is already there.

3. Provider registry and routing

  • Registry: In base.py or a small providers/registry.py, maintain a mapping vendor_id -> ModelProvider instance. Register provider instances at import/startup only—no static model lists. The registry holds only the code that knows how to talk to each vendor; model lists are always loaded live from the vendor API when GET /models is called. For each registered provider, GET /models calls provider.list_models(api_key), which performs an HTTP request to that vendor’s models API (e.g. Anthropic GET /v1/models, OpenAI GET /v1/models) and returns the result. Do not cache or store model lists in the registry or in config.
  • Resolve provider from model: Add a function get_provider_for_model(model_id: str) -> ModelProvider. Use model-id prefix (e.g. claude- for Anthropic, gpt- for OpenAI) to return the correct provider; unknown prefixes return None. Optionally support an explicit x-zergling-vendor header later; for this step prefix-based is enough so the REPL and web UI need no change.

4. Changes in main.py

  • Imports: Import the abstract types, get_provider_for_model, and the registry (so Anthropic is registered).
  • GET /models: Build result dict by iterating registered providers (or by vendor keys you want to expose). For each, call provider.list_models(api_key) and map provider.vendor_id → ListModelsResult serialized to the current JSON shape (models, error, has_more, etc.). Keep the same response shape so the UI and REPL remain compatible.

  • POST /chat:

    • Resolve provider: provider = get_provider_for_model(model); if None, 400 “Unknown model or vendor”.
    • API key / endpoint: today we have Anthropic-only env/headers. Pass the resolved key and optional endpoint into the provider (Anthropic provider can take api_key and optional endpoint; other vendors can have their own env vars later).
    • Get canonical tools from MCP if body.tool_names is set; filter by tool_names; call provider.to_tools(filtered_canonical).
    • Tool loop: messages list, max_tool_rounds = 10. Loop: result = await provider.chat(messages, model, system, max_tokens, tools). If result.content is set, return ChatResponse(content=result.content). If result.tool_uses is set, for each item run the MCP tool by name (same as today: mcp.get_tools(), tool.run(input)), build tool_result blocks, append assistant message with content list and user message with tool_result blocks to messages, then continue the loop. After max rounds, return 502.
  • Config / keys: Leave _server_has_anthropic_key() and /config as-is for now (still Anthropic-focused). Later you can generalize to “has API key for vendor X” when adding OpenAI.
Remove from main.py: inline Anthropic URL/endpoint constants used only by the provider, the direct Anthropic GET /models and POST /chat implementation, _anthropic_tools_from_names (replaced by provider.to_tools + filter by tool_names). Keep _tool_result_content, _get_api_key, _get_model, _parse_max_tokens, and request/response models.

5. Tool conversion and tool_names

  • Canonical tools come from get_tools_for_api(); filter by body.tool_names to the list of tool definitions for those names. Pass that filtered list to provider.to_tools(...). So the provider receives only the tools that are enabled for this request, in canonical form, and returns vendor-specific tools. The server then passes that vendor payload into provider.chat(..., tools=...).

6. REPL and Web UI

  • No changes required: they send x-zergling-model and optional x-zergling-api-key; the server routes by model id and returns the same response shapes.

7. Documentation and layout

  • Add a short note in zergling-agent-server/README.md that the server uses a provider abstraction and supports Anthropic and OpenAI; model id prefix is used to select the vendor. Document env vars for both (e.g. ZERGLING_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, OPENAI_API_KEY or ZERGLING_OPENAI_API_KEY).
  • If you add architecture/zergling-agent-server/ docs, add a sentence that GET /models and POST /chat are vendor-agnostic and implemented via the ModelProvider abstraction; model lists are loaded from each vendor’s API, not stored statically.

8. OpenAI provider

  • New module: zergling-agent-server/providers/openai.py.
  • list_models: Call OpenAI’s models API (e.g. GET https://api.openai.com/v1/models) with the provider’s API key. Map the response to ListModelsResult (id, and optionally display name / created, etc.). Handle pagination if the API supports it. Require API key (return error in result if missing).
  • chat: One round using OpenAI Chat Completions API. Accept messages, model, system, max_tokens, tools. Convert messages to OpenAI format (role + content). If tools is set, send them as tools in the request (function-calling format). Parse the response: if the choice has a message.content and no tool_calls, return ChatRoundResult(content=...). If the choice has message.tool_calls, map each to ToolUse(id, name, input) and return ChatRoundResult(tool_uses=[...]). Map OpenAI’s tool call id/name/arguments (JSON string) into the normalized ToolUse shape.
  • to_tools: Convert canonical {name, description, input_schema} to OpenAI’s function format: {"type": "function", "function": {"name": ..., "description": ..., "parameters": input_schema}}.
  • Config: Read API key from env OPENAI_API_KEY or ZERGLING_OPENAI_API_KEY (and optionally from a future header like x-zergling-openai-api-key). No static model list; models come from list_models() only.
  • Registry: Register OpenAIProvider() in the provider registry under vendor_id = "openai".
  • Routing: In get_provider_for_model, treat model ids that start with gpt- (or other known OpenAI prefixes, e.g. o1-) as OpenAI and return the OpenAI provider.
  • GET /models: Already iterates providers; the new OpenAI provider will be included automatically and its list_models(api_key) will be called to fill the openai key in the response (replacing the current placeholder empty list).

Summary of new/edited files

Optional follow-ups (out of scope for this plan)

  • Generalize /config to report per-vendor API key presence (e.g. has_anthropic_key, has_openai_key).
  • Add x-zergling-vendor header to force provider selection when model id is ambiguous.

Well that's lengthy as shit. At least you can read it though! There's still some rendering issues in there for sure, but we'll fix those later. It's getting late and I want to get on with the modeling. Let's make sure our vendor abstraction works and check out some code.

Model Provider Base Class
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

PROVIDER_REGISTRY: dict[str, "ModelProvider"] = {}


@dataclass
class ToolUse:
    id: str
    name: str
    input: dict


@dataclass
class ListModelsResult:
    models: list[dict] = field(default_factory=list)
    error: Optional[str] = None
    has_more: Optional[bool] = None
    first_id: Optional[str] = None
    last_id: Optional[str] = None


@dataclass
class ChatRoundResult:
    content: Optional[str] = None
    tool_uses: Optional[list[ToolUse]] = None


class ModelProvider(ABC):
    vendor_id: str = ""

    @abstractmethod
    async def list_models(self, api_key: Optional[str]) -> ListModelsResult:
        pass

    @abstractmethod
    async def chat(
        self,
        messages: list[dict],
        model: str,
        system: str,
        max_tokens: int,
        tools: Optional[list[dict]] = None,
        api_key: Optional[str] = None,
        endpoint: Optional[str] = None,
    ) -> ChatRoundResult:
        pass

    @abstractmethod
    def to_tools(self, canonical_tools: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
        pass


def register_provider(provider: ModelProvider) -> None:
    PROVIDER_REGISTRY[provider.vendor_id] = provider


def get_provider_for_model(model_id: str) -> Optional[ModelProvider]:
    if not model_id or not model_id.strip():
        return None
    mid = model_id.strip().lower()
    if mid.startswith("claude-"):
        return PROVIDER_REGISTRY.get("anthropic")
    if mid.startswith("gpt-") or mid.startswith("o1-") or mid.startswith("o3-"):
        return PROVIDER_REGISTRY.get("openai")
    return None

So far this doesn't look too bad. I appreciate the use of dataclasses to gather information on how models behave. I'm unsure as of right now what ListModelsResult will be, but we have a way to get a provider's list of models and chat with that model. Keeping this chat function as entirely functional (you have to pass in the api_key, endpoint, tools, etc. each time) may end up benefitting us in the long run as it will allow us to switch models easily. Essentially, if we can abstract away the differences between each provider and end up with a similar way to communicate with each one, including my local models, then we can select the right model for the job.

Restarting the app and server, everything seems to continue to work. Let's have it setup OpenAI as well and we'll see how well that works. Also, while we're at it, let's take a look at those architecture documents. OOOOOOHHHH, it didn't update the architecture directory! I've directed it to do this now. Seems like AGENTS.md may not have been picked up by Cursor? Maybe I'll make a rule in cursor to observe AGENTS.md if it exists. Excellent, it even updated existing files, though the documentation itself is quite bland and not instructive (docs below). I've added another rule to AGENTS.md:

  • Write documentation for your functions and classes
Model Providers Architecture Doc

Model Providers

Overview

Chat and model listing are vendor-agnostic: they go through a provider abstraction in providers/. The registry holds only provider instances; model lists are loaded from each vendor’s API when GET /models is called (no static model list). See web-server-and-api.md for how the API uses providers.

Interface

  • Module: providers/base.py defines the abstract base and shared types.
  • ModelProvider (ABC): vendor_id, list_models(api_key), chat(messages, model, system, max_tokens, tools, api_key?, endpoint?), to_tools(canonical_tools).
  • Result types: ListModelsResult (models, error?, pagination), ChatRoundResult (content? or tool_uses?), ToolUse (id, name, input). One round per chat() call; the server in main.py runs the tool loop (MCP execution and re-calling the provider).

Registry and routing

  • Registry: PROVIDER_REGISTRY in base.py; register_provider(provider) at import (see providers/__init__.py).
  • Routing: get_provider_for_model(model_id) selects by model-id prefix: claude- → Anthropic, gpt- / o1- / o3- → OpenAI. Unknown prefix returns None (API returns 400).

Implementations

Each provider converts canonical tools (name, description, input_schema from MCP) to its API format via to_tools(); the server passes the result into chat(..., tools=...).

Configuration

API keys are resolved in main.py per provider (header or env). Anthropic: ZERGLING_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY / ANTHROPIC_API_KEY. OpenAI: ZERGLING_OPENAI_API_KEY / OPENAI_API_KEY. Endpoint override (ZERGLING_API_ENDPOINT) applies to Anthropic only.

Okay, now the last piece for this article because it's getting late. I need a model selector in the chat app...and a dark mode theme! So let's instruct a new agent so it has 0 context from the prior agent's work and it has to use the architecture docs, and I'm going to add some special sauce to this prompt.

Update the zergling-web-ui to allow for model selection inside of the settings flyout. This should be a dropdown list of the models we can select. Query the server for supported models to ensure our list is up to date. Note that the server should use one API key header from the web ui, so if it requires different headers for anthropic and openai keys, fix that so they are the same header name. Once you are done with the model selection implementation. Add a theme to the settings menu as well. I want a theme that is a darker grey with white-ish text and pink as an accent color.
